environment.market_structure: The global laptop market is dominated by a handful of major players. Apple holds ~9% global PC market share but dominates the premium segment, generating $33.7B in annual Mac revenue. The market is segmented into budget, mid-range, and premium tiers. Apple's competitive advantage relies heavily on vertical integration — proprietary M-series silicon, macOS, and deep ecosystem lock-in with iPhone, iPad, AirPods, and iCloud. Switching costs are high for existing Apple users. The right-to-repair movement and growing consumer frustration with non-upgradeable, glued-together hardware have created an emerging counter-market of tech enthusiasts, developers, and environmentally conscious buyers who prioritize longevity and modularity over brand ecosystem.

incumbents.name: Apple Inc. (Mac Division)
incumbents.description: A $3.4T company with $33.7B in annual Mac revenue. Current MacBook lineup spans the MacBook Air (from $999) to MacBook Pro (up to $6,899). Competes on vertical integration — proprietary M-series chips deliver industry-leading performance per watt, and macOS tightly integrates with the broader Apple ecosystem. Apple has historically never engaged in price wars, instead relying on premium positioning, brand loyalty, and ecosystem lock-in to retain customers. Past competitive responses have included incremental spec bumps, occasional $100 price reductions on entry-level models, and expanding the lineup downward to capture education markets. Apple has actively fought right-to-repair legislation and designs products with soldered RAM, soldered storage, and proprietary components that cannot be user-serviced.

sme_move.description: Framework, a startup with ~$33M in annual revenue and 127 employees, launches an aggressive push into the mainstream laptop market. Their Laptop 13 starts at $769 and their Laptop 16 at $1,299 — both fully modular with user-swappable RAM, SSD, mainboard, GPU module, and expansion cards. Framework is positioning repairability and upgradeability as a direct alternative to Apple's closed ecosystem, targeting tech enthusiasts, developers, and environmentally conscious consumers who are frustrated with planned obsolescence. Their marketing emphasizes that a single Framework laptop can last a decade through component upgrades, versus Apple's 4-5 year replacement cycle.
sme_move.move_type: Modular product differentiation targeting the premium segment's pain points
